title: Field-Dependence and Lateralization of Function in the Human Brain
abstract:
Field-Dependence and Lateralization
of Function in the Human Brain
Bertram D. Cohen, PhD, Piscataway, NJ; Stanley Berent, PhD, Charlottesville, Va;
and Albert J. Silverman, MD, Ann Arbor, Mich
Rod and frame tests were administered before and after a single
electroconvulsive shock treatment (ECT) delivered either to the left
or right cerebral hemisphere of neurologically normal patients being
treated for depression. A third group was also tested twice but with
no intervening ECT. All 12 left-ECT patients showed more field-de-
pendence (larger errors) on the second test; all 12 right ECT patients
showed less field-dependence. The findings were discussed in terms
of a model in which two cognitive control processes, field articula-
tion and scanning, are localized in the left and right brain hemi-
spheres, respectively. Also, implications were drawn regarding the
possibly special role of shock to the right hemisphere in the treat-
ment of depression.
When
a
vertical
rod
is
viewed within
a tilted
square frame in an otherwise blacked out environ¬
ment, there is a tendency to misperceive the rod as tilted
due to the influence of the frame. Stable individual differ¬
ences in this tendency, termed field-dependence, were ob¬
served in early studies by Witkin and Asch (1948).' In
1954, Witkin et al2 showed also that field-dependence, as
measured by the rod and frame test, was correlated with a
broad range of other characteristics of personality and
performance. More recently A. J. Silverman (1969),3 hav¬
ing reviewed a number of studies showing psychophysio-
logical differences between extreme field-dependent and
field-independent persons, speculated that extreme field-
dependence might signify a subclinical cerebral injury.
As a follow-up on the Silverman speculation, Berent and
Silverman
(1972)4 administered,
to groups of college
women varying in rod and frame estimates of field-depen¬
dence, a battery of special tests considered sensitive to
cerebral pathology. The particular tests used by Berent
and Silverman included a verbal (words) and a visual
(forms) paired-associate learning task. These tasks had
been used earlier by Starck (1961)5 to discriminate success¬
fully between patients with known brain damage localized
in either the left or right cerebral hemisphere. Stark had
shown that left-hemisphere patients performed poorly on
the words but not the forms task (compared to right-hemi¬
sphere or to nonbrain-damaged patients); in contrast,
right-hemisphere patients performed poorly on the forms
but not the words task. The words and forms tasks had
also been administered by Cohen et al (1968)°· to neurolog-
ically normal depressed patients before and after a series
of unilateral electroconvulsive shock treatments (ECT) de¬
livered either to the left or to the right cerebral hemi¬
sphere. Their findings corresponded with those of Stark;
left-hemisphere-shocked patients showed a words deficit
and right-hemisphere patients, a forms deficit. The Stark
and the Cohen et al pattern of findings relating verbal
and visuoperceptive deficits to left- and right-hemisphere
dysfunction, respectively, is in keeping with the results of
many other psychoneurological investigations of later-
alized brain function (see Yates7 and Gazzaniga8).
Given the nonverbal, visuoperceptive character of the
rod and frame test, Berent and Silverman had expected
their extreme field-dependent group to show inferior per¬
formance specifically on the forms task, a finding that
would have been consistent with a hypothesis of right-
hemisphere dysfunction underlying extreme field-depen¬
dent performance. However, their results showed instead
that the extreme field-dependent group was significantly
inferior on the words and not the forms task (relative to
middle range and field-independent groups),
a finding
consistent with a left-hemisphere hypothesis.
The present investigation was designed to provide a di¬
rect test of the relation between lateralized cerebral dys¬
function and field-dependent performance on the rod and
frame test. Specifically, this study compares the effects on
rod and frame performance of a single ECT administered
either to the right or left cerebral hemisphere of patients
being treated for depression. If left-hemisphere dysfunc¬
tion is specifically implicated in extreme field-dependence,
then left-hemisphere ECT, rather than right, should result
in increased rod and frame error scores. This technique
(unilateral ECT) is essentially the same as we used in our
previous study of brain-function lateralization.6
Method
Subjects.—The patients were all right-handed women between
the ages of 19 and 59 admitted for ECT to the inpatient service of
the Carrier Clinic, Belle Mead, NJ. All were diagnosed as de¬
pressed on admission and showed no evidence of alcoholism or
neurological disease. All were judged physically healthy according
to physical examination, medical history, electroencephalogram,
Accepted for publication Nov 20, 1972.
From the Rutgers Medical School (Dr. Cohen), Piscataway, NJ; Univer-
sity of Virginia School of Medicine (Dr. Berent), Charlottesville; and the
University of Michigan Medical School (Dr. Silverman), Ann Arbor.
Reprint requests to Department of Psychiatry, Rutgers Medical School,
University Heights, Piscataway, NJ 08854 (Dr. Cohen).

 chest x-ray film, and other routine laboratory tests.
Treatment Groups.—Shortly after admission, each patient was
given the rod and frame test described below, and then randomly
assigned to one of two ECT groups: left-ECT and right-ECT. The
ECT was scheduled to take place between 24 and 48 hours after
the first rod and frame test. The rod and frame test was then ad¬
ministered again five hours after ECT.
If, because of circumstances not attributable to her clinical
status (for example, laboratory test results not ready), the patient
was not given her first ECT at the scheduled time, she was as¬
signed to a no-ECT control group. The second rod and frame test
was then administered five hours after the scheduled, but missed,
ECT.
This sampling procedure was continued until the three groups
contained twelve patients each. Although this resulted in some ap¬
parent discrepancy among the groups in average age-left-ECT,
33.1 years; right-ECT, 41.6 years; and control, 42.2 years-the be-
tween-groups variation was not satistically significant according
to a Kruskal-Wallis one-way analysis of variance (H = 4.82, df = 2).
Nor was there any significant variation among the groups in so-
cioeconomic status (H = 2.31, df=2) of the patients' immediate
families as measured by occupational ratings.9 Finally, there was
no significant variation among the groups in pre-ECT rod and
frame scores (H = .18, df =2).
Rod and Frame Test.—The rod and frame apparatus and proce¬
dures have been described in detail elsewhere.10 The method re¬
quires the subject to direct the experimenter's turning of the rod
until the subject considers it to be vertical (parallel to the walls of
the room). A series of eight rod and frame starting positions was
presented with 28° deviations used throughout. A patient's score
for a given test was the average of her deviations from 0° over
the eight settings.
Unilateral ECT.-A standard ECT machine set at 150v for 0.6-sec-
ond duration was used to administer all treatments. Anesthesia
consisted of thiopental sodium. Succinylcholine chloride was also
given routinely as a muscle relaxant. Unilateral electrode place-
ments were as described by Lancaster et al." The clinician admin¬
istering treatment judged every patient to have had a grand mal
Results
The mean rod and frame scores for each group on the
first and second tests are given in the Table. These find¬
ings show that the left-ECT group became more field-de¬
pendent following treatment; that is, these patients re¬
sponded with larger errors in their post-ECT than in their
pre-ECT positionings of the rod. In contrast, the findings
show that right-hemisphere treatment resulted in de¬
creased field-dependence; the right-ECT patients' rod
placements became more accurate following treatment.
The control patients differed from both ECT groups in
that they showed little or no change from the first to the
second test. An analysis of variance applied to the Table
data showed highly significant groups (left, right, no
ECT) by test (first, second) interaction {F (2,33)
= 17.46,  
< .001).
The extraordinary consistency of these findings is evi¬
dent in the Figure. All 12 left-ECT cases showed incre-

first and second tests are given in the Table. These find¬

pendent following treatment; that is, these patients re¬
sponded with larger errors in their post-ECT than in their
pre-ECT positionings of the rod. In contrast, the findings
show that right-hemisphere treatment resulted in de¬
creased field-dependence; the right-ECT patients' rod
placements became more accurate following treatment.

tion from vertical) from first, or pre-ECT, to second, or post-ECT,
tests. Increases in error are shown by positive scores and decreases

showed decrements, but these were minuscule compared
to the right-ECT patients: only one right-ECT patient's
decrement was small enough to fall inside the control
Consideration was given to the possibility that the find¬
ings were influenced by variations in the patients' ages.
However, the obtained correlation between age and the
change from first to second rod and frame error score was
very low and nonsignificant (r
The findings clearly demonstrate that increased field-
dependence, as measured by the rod and frame test, is a
regular effect of left-cerebral-hemisphere ECT, consistent
with expectations based on.our analysis of the Berent-Sil-
verman4 and Cohen et al6 results. However, the equally
striking finding of decreased field-dependence (more accu¬
rate positioning of the rod) consequent to right-hemi¬
sphere ECT came as a complete surprise.
It may seem paradoxical that in our earlier study6 right-
hemisphere ECT impaired performance on the forms task
while in the present study it improved performance on the
rod and frame task, since both tasks involve
perceptive skills. A possible resolution of this apparent
contradiction is suggested by a more detailed comparison
of the two tasks. The forms task requires the subject to in¬
spect each of a series of designs so that one part of a given
design can later be reproduced in relation to the other. In
order for this task to be performed successfully, attention
must be distributed over all parts of the design. In con¬
trast, it is possible to achieve accuracy on the rod and
frame task by attending only to the most focal element of
the stimulus configuration, the rod, and ignoring the pe¬
ripheral elements, the frame. In fact, if a subject attends
to the entire field, he will sample stimulus elements that
normally interfere with veridical perception in this situ¬
An important possible implication of this analysis is
that right-hemisphere ECT attenuates the patients' re¬
sponsiveness to a stimulus field so that the more periph¬
eral elements of the field fall below
threshold." If so, the shock, in effect, tends to remove
(functionally) the main source of interference with accu¬
rate perception in the rod and frame task, namely, the
frame. A phenomenon of this type has been described by
entiation." In this phenomenon there is a tendency to
achieve low-error rod and frame
ceptually differentiating rod from frame but simply by at¬
tending only to that portion of the stimulus field empha¬
sized in the instructions and thereby "registering few
elements of the distracting background stimulation."l2(pl21)
Although frankly speculative, it is possible to integrate
our findings for the right and left cerebral hemispheres,
respectively, by relating them to the two "cognitive con¬
trols of attention deployment" termed by
scanning and field articulation. The scanning control,
which is thought to determine the extensiveness with
which an individual samples data from a stimulus field,
would appear to be mediated by structures of the right
hemisphere. In contrast, the field articulation control—the
meaningful sorting-out of elements of the field relative to
a given task—would seem to be mediated by structures of
the left hemisphere. Both control functions were
ceived by Gardner as central processes "relevant to situ¬
ations in which the subject must attend (a) to stimuli out¬
side himself or, (6) to elements of memory schemata
It is tempting to infer from our analysis the possible
crucial role of shock to the right cerebral hemisphere in
bringing about the mood modifying (antidepressant) ef¬
fects of ECT. That is, the postulated field-attenuation ef¬
fects of right-hemisphere ECT might result in reduced at¬
tention not only to external stimulus elements, as in the
rod and frame situation, but also to internal "elements of
memory schemata" such as the dysphoric preoccupations
that often intrude upon and interfere with the everyday-
life performances of depressed patients. Trends noted in
clinical studies comparing the antidepressant effect of
right- and left-hemisphere ECT," and right- and left-in-
tracarotid injections of amobarbital sodium,15 although
hardly conclusive, have been consistent with our right-
hemisphere hypothesis. This is an area in which carefully
designed studies may well yield important, practical re¬

The professional staff and patients of the Carrier Clinic, Belle Mead, NJ
cooperated in the execution of this study.
